(English: [/ˈɒksɪtən, -tæn, -tɑːn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) Occitan pronunciation: [[utsiˈta, uksiˈta]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan")),[[a]](#cite_note-14) also known by its native speakers as ***lenga d'òc*** (Occitan: [[ˈleŋɡɒ ˈðɔ(k)]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Oc-Lenga_d%27%C3%B2c.ogg "File:Oc-Lenga d'òc.ogg"); [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *langue d'oc*), sometimes also referred to as **Provençal**, is a [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") spoken in southern France, [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), Italy's [Occitan Valleys](/wiki/Occitan_Valleys "Occitan Valleys"), as well as in the [Catalonian](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") [Val d'Aran](/wiki/Val_d%27Aran "Val d'Aran"); collectively, these regions are sometimes referred to as [Occitania](/wiki/Occitania "Occitania"). It is also spoken in the southern Italian province of [Cosenza](/wiki/Province_of_Cosenza "Province of Cosenza") (mostly in [Guardia Piemontese](/wiki/Guardia_Piemontese "Guardia Piemontese")). There it is referred to as [Gardiol](/wiki/Gardiol_language "Gardiol language"), which is considered a separate Occitanic language.[[14]](#cite_note-15) While this theory has been disproved, French scholars included [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") as a dialect of Occitan, as the [linguistic distance](/wiki/Linguistic_distance "Linguistic distance") between this language and some Occitan dialects (such as the [Gascon language](/wiki/Gascon_language "Gascon language")) is similar to the distance between different Occitan dialects. Catalan was considered a dialect of Occitan until the end of the 19th century[[15]](#cite_note-16) and still today remains its closest relative.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Occitan has a particularly rich lexicon. *Lo Panoccinari*, considered the most comprehensive dictionary ever published in this language, records over 250,000 unique words[[17]](#cite_note-18) (more than 310,000 including dialectal variations).

**Occitan** (English: [/ˈɒksɪtən, -tæn, -tɑːn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) Occitan pronunciation: [[utsiˈta, uksiˈta]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan")),[[a]](#cite_note-14) also known by its native speakers as ***lenga d'òc*** (Occitan: [[ˈleŋɡɒ ˈðɔ(k)]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Oc-Lenga_d%27%C3%B2c.ogg "File:Oc-Lenga d'òc.ogg"); [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *langue d'oc*), sometimes also referred to as **Provençal**, is a [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") spoken in southern France, [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), Italy's [Occitan Valleys](/wiki/Occitan_Valleys "Occitan Valleys"), as well as in the [Catalonian](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") [Val d'Aran](/wiki/Val_d%27Aran "Val d'Aran"); collectively, these regions are sometimes referred to as [Occitania](/wiki/Occitania "Occitania"). It is also spoken in the southern Italian province of [Cosenza](/wiki/Province_of_Cosenza "Province of Cosenza") (mostly in [Guardia Piemontese](/wiki/Guardia_Piemontese "Guardia Piemontese")). There it is referred to as [Gardiol](/wiki/Gardiol_language "Gardiol language"), which is considered a separate Occitanic language.[[14]](#cite_note-15) While this theory has been disproved, French scholars included [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") as a dialect of Occitan, as the [linguistic distance](/wiki/Linguistic_distance "Linguistic distance") between this language and some Occitan dialects (such as the [Gascon language](/wiki/Gascon_language "Gascon language")) is similar to the distance between different Occitan dialects. Catalan was considered a dialect of Occitan until the end of the 19th century[[15]](#cite_note-16) and still today remains its closest relative.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Occitan has a particularly rich lexicon. *Lo Panoccinari*, considered the most comprehensive dictionary ever published in this language, records over 250,000 unique words[[17]](#cite_note-18) (more than 310,000 including dialectal variations).

Occitan is an official language of Catalonia, where a subdialect of Gascon known as [Aranese](/wiki/Aranese_dialect "Aranese dialect") is spoken (in the [Val d'Aran](/wiki/Val_d%27Aran "Val d'Aran")).[[18]](#cite_note-19) Since September 2010, the [Parliament of Catalonia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Catalonia "Parliament of Catalonia") has considered Aranese Occitan to be the officially preferred language for use in the Val d'Aran.

Across history, the terms [Limousin](/wiki/Limousin_dialect "Limousin dialect") (*Lemosin*), [Languedocien](/wiki/Languedocien_dialect "Languedocien dialect") (*Lengadocian*), [Gascon](/wiki/Gascon_dialect "Gascon dialect"), in addition to [Provençal](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al_dialect "Provençal dialect") (*Provençal*, *Provençau* or *Prouvençau*) later have been used as synonyms for the whole of Occitan; nowadays, the term "Provençal" is understood mainly as the Occitan dialect spoken in [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence"), in southeast France.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Unlike other Romance languages such as French or Spanish, Occitan does not have a single written standard form, nor does it have official status in France, home to most of its speakers. Instead, there are competing norms for writing Occitan, some of which attempt to be pan-dialectal, whereas others are based on a particular dialect. These efforts are hindered by the rapidly declining use of Occitan as a spoken language in much of southern France, as well as by the significant differences in phonology and vocabulary among different Occitan dialects.

According to the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [Red Book of Endangered Languages](/wiki/Red_Book_of_Endangered_Languages "Red Book of Endangered Languages"),[[20]](#cite_note-21) four of the six major dialects of Occitan (Provençal, [Auvergnat](/wiki/Auvergnat_dialect "Auvergnat dialect"), Limousin and Languedocien) are considered [severely endangered](/wiki/Endangered_language#Defining_and_measuring_endangerment "Endangered language"), whereas the remaining two ([Gascon](/wiki/Gascon_language "Gascon language") and [Vivaro-Alpine](/wiki/Vivaro-Alpine_dialect "Vivaro-Alpine dialect")) are considered [definitely endangered](/wiki/Endangered_language#Defining_and_measuring_endangerment "Endangered language").

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

### History of the modern term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History of the modern term")]

The name Occitan comes from the term *lenga d'òc* ("language of òc"), *òc* being the Occitan word for *yes.* While the term would have been in use orally for some time after the decline of Latin, as far as historical records show, the Italian medieval poet [Dante](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") was the first to have recorded the term *lingua d'oc* in writing. In his *[De vulgari eloquentia](/wiki/De_vulgari_eloquentia "De vulgari eloquentia")*, he wrote in Latin, *"nam alii oc, alii si, alii vero dicunt oil"* ("for some say *òc*, others *sì*, yet others say *oïl*"), thereby highlighting three major [Romance literary languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") that were well known in Italy, based on each language's word for "yes", the **òc* language* (Occitan), the **oïl* language* (French), and the **sì* language* (Italian).

The word *òc* came from [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") *hoc* ("this"), while *oïl* originated from Latin *hoc illud* ("this [is] it"). [Old Catalan](/wiki/Old_Catalan "Old Catalan") and now the Catalan of Northern Catalonia also have *hoc* (*òc*). Other Romance languages derive their word for "yes" from the Latin *sic*, "thus [it is], [it was done], etc.", such as Spanish *sí*, [Eastern Lombard](/wiki/Eastern_Lombard_dialect "Eastern Lombard dialect") *sé*, Italian *sì*, or Portuguese *sim*. In modern Catalan, as in modern Spanish, *sí* is usually used as a response, although the language retains the word *oi*, akin to *òc*, which is sometimes used at the end of [yes–no questions](/wiki/Yes%E2%80%93no_question "Yes–no question") and also in higher register as a positive response.[[21]](#cite_note-22) French uses *si* to answer "yes" in response to questions that are asked in the negative sense: for example, *"Vous n'avez pas de frères?" "Si, j'en ai sept."* ("You don't have any brothers, do you?" "Yes I do, I have seven.").

The name "Occitan" was attested around 1300 as *occitanus*, a crossing of *oc* and *aquitanus* ([Aquitanian](/wiki/Aquitania "Aquitania")).[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Other names for Occitan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Other names for Occitan")]

For many centuries, the Occitan dialects (together with [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"))[[23]](#cite_note-24) were referred to as *Limousin* or *Provençal*, after the names of two regions lying within the modern Occitan-speaking area. After [Frédéric Mistral](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Mistral "Frédéric Mistral")'s [Félibrige](/wiki/F%C3%A9librige "Félibrige") movement in the 19th century, Provençal achieved the greatest literary recognition and so became the most popular term for Occitan.

According to [Joseph Anglade](/wiki/Joseph_Anglade "Joseph Anglade"), a [philologist](/wiki/Philology "Philology") and specialist of [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") literature who helped impose the then [archaic](/wiki/Archaism "Archaism") term *Occitan* as the standard name,[[24]](#cite_note-25) the word *Lemosin* was first used to designate the language at the beginning of the 13th century by [Catalan](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") [troubadour](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour") [Raimon Vidal de Besalú(n)](/wiki/Raimon_Vidal_de_Bezaudun "Raimon Vidal de Bezaudun") in his *Razós de trobar*:

> *La parladura Francesca val mais et [es] plus avinenz a far romanz e pasturellas; mas cella de Lemozin val mais per far vers et cansons et serventés; et per totas las terras de nostre lengage son de major autoritat li cantar de la lenga Lemosina que de negun'autra parladura, per qu'ieu vos en parlarai primeramen.*[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnglade1921[httpgallicabnffrark12148bpt6k255748jf43image_7]-26)
>
> The French language is worthier and better suited for romances and [pastourelles](/wiki/Pastourelle "Pastourelle"); but [the language] from [Limousin](/wiki/History_of_Limousin "History of Limousin") is of greater value for writing poems and [cançons](/wiki/Canso_(song) "Canso (song)") and [sirventés](/wiki/Sirventes "Sirventes"); and across the whole of the lands where our tongue is spoken, the literature in the Limousin language has more authority than any other dialect, wherefore I shall use this name in priority.

The term *Provençal*, though implying a reference to the region of [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence"), historically was used for Occitan as a whole, for "in the eleventh, the twelfth, and sometimes also the thirteenth centuries, one would understand under the name of Provence the whole territory of the old [Provincia romana Gallia Narbonensis](/wiki/Gallia_Narbonensis "Gallia Narbonensis") and even [Aquitaine](/wiki/Gallia_Aquitania "Gallia Aquitania")".[[26]](#cite_note-27) The term first came into fashion in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy").[[27]](#cite_note-28)

Currently, [linguists](/wiki/Linguist "Linguist") use the terms *Provençal* and *Limousin* strictly to refer to specific varieties within Occitan, using *Occitan* for the language as a whole. Many non-specialists, however, continue to refer to the language as *Provençal*.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Old Occitan](/wiki/Old_Occitan "Old Occitan") and [Occitan literature](/wiki/Occitan_literature "Occitan literature")

One of the oldest written fragments of the language found dates back to 960, shown here in italics mixed with non-italicized Latin:

> De ista hora in antea non *decebrà* Ermengaus filius Eldiarda Froterio episcopo filio Girberga *ne* Raimundo filio Bernardo vicecomite de castello de Cornone ... *no·l li tolrà ni no·l li devedarà ni no l'en decebrà* ... nec societatem non *aurà*, si per castellum recuperare *non o fa*, et si recuperare potuerit in potestate Froterio et Raimundo *lo tornarà*, per ipsas horas quæ Froterius et Raimundus *l'en comonrà*.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

[Carolingian](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") [litanies](/wiki/Litany "Litany") (c. 780), though the leader sang in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), were [answered](/wiki/Response_(liturgy) "Response (liturgy)") to in Old Occitan by the people (*Ora pro nos*; *Tu lo juva*).[[29]](#cite_note-30)

Other famous pieces include the *[Boecis](/wiki/Boecis "Boecis")*, a 258-line-long poem written entirely in the Limousin dialect of Occitan between the year 1000 and 1030 and inspired by [Boethius](/wiki/Boethius "Boethius")'s *[The Consolation of Philosophy](/wiki/The_Consolation_of_Philosophy "The Consolation of Philosophy")*; the [Waldensian](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians") *[La nobla leyczon](/wiki/La_nobla_leyczon "La nobla leyczon")* (dated 1100),[[30]](#cite_note-31) **[Cançó de Santa Fe](/wiki/Can%C3%A7%C3%B3_de_Santa_Fe "Cançó de Santa Fe")** (c. 1054–1076), the *[Romance of Flamenca](/wiki/Romance_of_Flamenca "Romance of Flamenca")* (13th century), the *[Song of the Albigensian Crusade](/wiki/Song_of_the_Albigensian_Crusade "Song of the Albigensian Crusade")* (1213–1219?), *[Daurel e Betó](/wiki/Daurel_e_Bet%C3%B3 "Daurel e Betó")* (12th or 13th century), *[Las, qu'i non-sun sparvir, astur](/w/index.php?title=Las,_qu%27i_non-sun_sparvir,_astur&action=edit&redlink=1 "Las, qu'i non-sun sparvir, astur (page does not exist)")* (11th century) and *[Tomida femina](/wiki/Tomida_femina "Tomida femina")* (9th or 10th century).

Occitan was the vehicle for the influential [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry") of the medieval [troubadours](/wiki/Troubadours "Troubadours") (*trobadors*) and *[trobairitz](/wiki/Trobairitz "Trobairitz")*: At that time, the language was understood and celebrated throughout most of educated Europe.[[31]](#cite_note-32) It was the maternal language of the English queen [Eleanor of Aquitaine](/wiki/Eleanor_of_Aquitaine "Eleanor of Aquitaine") and kings [Richard I](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England") (who wrote troubadour poetry) and [John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England").

With the gradual imposition of French royal power over its territory, Occitan declined in status from the 14th century on. The [Ordinance of Villers-Cotterêts](/wiki/Ordinance_of_Villers-Cotter%C3%AAts "Ordinance of Villers-Cotterêts") (1539) decreed that the *langue d'oïl* (French – though at the time referring to the [Francien language](/wiki/Francien_language "Francien language") and not the larger collection of dialects grouped under the name [langues d'oïl](/wiki/Langues_d%27o%C3%AFl "Langues d'oïl")) should be used for all French administration. Occitan's greatest decline occurred during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), in which diversity of language was considered a threat.

In 1903, the four Gospels ("Lis Evangèli", i.e. Matthew, Mark, Luke and John) were translated into Provençal as spoken in Cannes and Grasse. The translation was given the official Roman Catholic Imprimatur by vicar general A. Estellon.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The literary renaissance of the late 19th century (in which the 1904 [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") winner, [Frédéric Mistral](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Mistral "Frédéric Mistral"), among others, was involved) was attenuated by [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), when (in addition to the disruption caused by any major war) many Occitan speakers spent extended periods of time alongside French-speaking comrades.

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Origins")]

Because the geographical territory in which Occitan is spoken is surrounded by regions in which other [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") are used, external influences may have influenced its origin and development. Many factors favored its development as its own language.

* Mountains and seas: The range of Occitan is naturally bounded by the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean"), [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic "Atlantic"), [Massif Central](/wiki/Massif_Central "Massif Central"), [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), and [Pyrenees](/wiki/Pyrenees "Pyrenees"), respectively.
* Buffer zones: [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid") land, [marshes](/wiki/Marsh "Marsh"), and areas otherwise impractical for farming and resistant of colonization provide further separation (territory between [Loire](/wiki/Loire "Loire") and [Garonne](/wiki/Garonne "Garonne"), the [Aragon](/wiki/Aragon "Aragon") desert plateau).
* Constant populations: Some Occitan-speaking peoples are descended from people living in the region since prehistoric times.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBec1963-33)
* Deeper [Roman](/wiki/Roman_people "Roman people") influence: The Romans had established an earlier presence in Southern France in 121 BC beginning with [Gallia Narbonensis](/wiki/Gallia_Narbonensis "Gallia Narbonensis"), where the seeds of the Occitan language were first sown. According to Müller, "France's linguistic separation began with Roman influence"[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBec196320–21-34)
* A separate [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon"): Although Occitan is midway between the [Gallo-Romance](/wiki/Gallo-Romance_languages "Gallo-Romance languages") and [Iberian Romance languages](/wiki/Iberian_Romance_languages "Iberian Romance languages"), it has "around 550 words inherited from Latin that no longer exist in the langues d'oïl or in [Franco-Provençal](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al_language "Franco-Provençal language")"[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBec196320–21-34)
* Lack of [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") influence: "The [Frankish](/wiki/Frankish_language "Frankish language") lexicon and its phonetic influence often end above the *oc*/*oïl* line"[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBec196320–21-34)

### Occitan in the Iberian Peninsula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Occitan in the Iberian Peninsula")]

Catalan in Spain's northern and central Mediterranean coastal regions and the [Balearic Islands](/wiki/Balearic_Islands "Balearic Islands") is closely related to Occitan, sharing many linguistic features and a common origin (see [Occitano-Romance languages](/wiki/Occitano-Romance_languages "Occitano-Romance languages")). The language was one of the first to gain prestige as a medium for literature among Romance languages in the Middle Ages. Indeed, in the 12th and 13th centuries, Catalan troubadours such as [Guerau de Cabrera](/wiki/Guerau_III_de_Cabrera "Guerau III de Cabrera"), Guilhem de Bergadan, Guilhem de Cabestany, [Huguet de Mataplana](/wiki/Huguet_de_Mataplana "Huguet de Mataplana"), Raimon Vidal de Besalú, [Cerverí de Girona](/wiki/Cerver%C3%AD_de_Girona "Cerverí de Girona"), Formit de Perpinhan, and [Jofre de Foixà](/wiki/Jofre_de_Foix%C3%A0 "Jofre de Foixà") wrote in Occitan.

At the end of the 11th century, the *[Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks")*, as they were called at the time, started to penetrate the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") through the [Ways of St. James](/wiki/Way_of_St._James "Way of St. James") via [Somport](/wiki/Somport "Somport") and [Roncesvalles](/wiki/Roncesvalles "Roncesvalles"), settling in various locations in the Kingdoms of [Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre") and [Aragon](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aragon "Kingdom of Aragon") enticed by the privileges granted them by the [Navarrese kings](/wiki/List_of_Navarrese_monarchs "List of Navarrese monarchs"). They settled in large groups, forming ethnic [boroughs](/wiki/Borough "Borough") where Occitan was used for everyday life, in [Pamplona](/wiki/Pamplona "Pamplona"), [Sangüesa](/wiki/Sang%C3%BCesa "Sangüesa"), and [Estella-Lizarra](/wiki/Estella-Lizarra "Estella-Lizarra"), among others.[[34]](#cite_note-35) These boroughs in Navarre may have been close-knit communities that tended not to assimilate with the predominantly [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language")-speaking general population. Their language became the status language chosen by the Navarrese kings, nobility, and upper classes for official and trade purposes in the period stretching from the early 13th century to the late 14th century.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

Written administrative records were in a *[koiné](/wiki/Koin%C3%A9_language "Koiné language")* based on the Languedocien dialect from Toulouse with fairly archaic linguistic features. Evidence survives of a written account in Occitan from Pamplona centered on the [burning](/wiki/Death_by_burning "Death by burning") of borough San Nicolas from 1258, while the *History of the War of Navarre* by Guilhem Anelier (1276), albeit written in Pamplona, shows a linguistic variant from [Toulouse](/wiki/History_of_Toulouse#13th_to_14th_century "History of Toulouse").[[36]](#cite_note-37)

Things turned out slightly otherwise in Aragon, where the sociolinguistic situation was different, with a clearer Basque-Romance bilingual situation (cf. Basques from the Val d'Aran cited c. 1000), but a receding Basque language (Basque banned in the marketplace of Huesca, 1349).[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) While the language was chosen as a medium of prestige in records and official statements along with Latin in the early 13th century, Occitan faced competition from the rising local Romance vernacular, the [Navarro-Aragonese](/wiki/Navarro-Aragonese "Navarro-Aragonese"), both orally and in writing, especially after Aragon's territorial conquests south to [Zaragoza](/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza"), [Huesca](/wiki/Huesca "Huesca") and [Tudela](/wiki/Tudela,_Navarre "Tudela, Navarre") between 1118 and 1134. It resulted that a second Occitan immigration of this period was assimilated by the similar [Navarro-Aragonese language](/wiki/Navarro-Aragonese "Navarro-Aragonese"), which at the same time was fostered and chosen by the kings of [Aragon](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Aragon "Kingdom of Aragon"). In the 14th century, Occitan across the whole southern Pyrenean area fell into decay and became largely absorbed into [Navarro-Aragonese](/wiki/Navarro-Aragonese "Navarro-Aragonese") first and [Castilian](/wiki/Castilian_Spanish "Castilian Spanish") later in the 15th century, after their exclusive boroughs broke up (1423, [Pamplona](/wiki/Pamplona#Three_boroughs_and_one_city "Pamplona")'s boroughs unified).[[39]](#cite_note-40)

Gascon-speaking communities were called to move in for trading purposes by Navarrese kings in the early 12th century to the coastal fringe extending from [San Sebastian](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "San Sebastián") to the river [Bidasoa](/wiki/Bidasoa "Bidasoa"), where they settled down. The language variant they used was different from the ones in Navarre, i.e. a [Béarnese dialect](/wiki/B%C3%A9arnese_dialect "Béarnese dialect") of Gascon.[[40]](#cite_note-dialnet.unirioja.es-41) Gascon remained in use in this area far longer than in Navarre and Aragon, until the 19th century, thanks mainly to the fact that Donostia and [Pasaia](/wiki/Pasaia "Pasaia") maintained close ties with [Bayonne](/wiki/Bayonne "Bayonne").

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

### Number of speakers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Number of speakers")]

The area where Occitan was historically dominant has approximately 16 million inhabitants. Recent research has shown it may be spoken as a first language by approximately 789,000 people[[2]](#cite_note-bernissan-2)[[3]](#cite_note-martel-3) in [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"). In Monaco, Occitan coexists with [Monégasque](/wiki/Mon%C3%A9gasque_language "Monégasque language") [Ligurian](/wiki/Ligurian_(Romance_language) "Ligurian (Romance language)"), which is the other native language.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) Up to seven million people in France understand the language,[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46) whereas twelve to fourteen million fully spoke it in 1921.[[46]](#cite_note-47) In 1860, Occitan speakers represented more than 39%[[47]](#cite_note-48) of the whole French population (52% for [francophones](/wiki/Francophone "Francophone") proper); they were still 26% to 36% in the 1920s,[[48]](#cite_note-49) but less than 7% in 1993.

### Usage in France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Usage in France")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Place_Mage_%28Toulouse%29_-_plaques.jpg/330px-Place_Mage_%28Toulouse%29_-_plaques.jpg)](/wiki/File:Place_Mage_(Toulouse)_-_plaques.jpg)

This bilingual street sign in [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse"), like many such signs found in Toulouse's historical districts, is maintained primarily for its antique charm, and is typical of what little remains of the *lenga d'òc* in southern French cities.

Though it was still an everyday language for most of the rural population of southern France well into the 20th century, the language is now declining in every region where it was spoken.

A 2020 study[[49]](#cite_note-50) conducted by the Office Public de la Langue Occitane on the territories of the Nouvelle-Aquitaine and Occitanie regions estimated around 540,000 speakers in these two regions. It is worth noting that the survey was conducted in the Occitan language for respondents who declared they were proficient in it. However, the regions including Auvergne and Provence were effectively excluded from this census, as the Office in question does not currently have a partnership with these territories.

According to the 1999 census, there were 610,000 native speakers (almost all of whom were also native French speakers) and perhaps another million people with some exposure to the language. Following the pattern of [language shift](/wiki/Language_shift "Language shift"), most of this remainder is to be found among the eldest populations. Occitan activists (called *Occitanists*) have attempted, in particular with the advent of Occitan-language preschools (the *[Calandretas](/wiki/Calandreta "Calandreta")*), to reintroduce the language to the young.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

Nonetheless, the number of proficient speakers of Occitan is thought to be dropping precipitously. A tourist in the cities in southern France is unlikely to hear a single Occitan word spoken on the street (or, for that matter, in a home), and is likely to only find the occasional vestige, such as street signs (and, of those, most will have French equivalents more prominently displayed), to remind them of the traditional language of the area.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Occitan speakers, as a result of generations of systematic suppression and humiliation (see [Vergonha](/wiki/Vergonha "Vergonha")), seldom use the language in the presence of strangers, whether they are from abroad or from outside Occitania (in this case, often merely and abusively referred to as *Parisiens* or *Nordistes*, which means *northerners*). Occitan is still spoken by many elderly people in rural areas, but they generally switch to French when dealing with outsiders.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

Occitan's decline is somewhat less pronounced in [Béarn](/wiki/B%C3%A9arn "Béarn") because of the province's history (a late addition to the Kingdom of France), though even there the language is little spoken outside the homes of the rural elderly. The village of [Artix](/wiki/Artix,_Pyr%C3%A9n%C3%A9es-Atlantiques "Artix, Pyrénées-Atlantiques") is notable for having elected to post street signs in the local language.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

### Usage outside France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Usage outside France")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Europe2005_bossost-aranes1.jpg/250px-Europe2005_bossost-aranes1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europe2005_bossost-aranes1.jpg)

Aranese signage in [Bossòst](/wiki/Boss%C3%B2st "Bossòst"), [Val d'Aran](/wiki/Val_d%27Aran "Val d'Aran"), Spain

* In the Val d'Aran, in the northwest corner of [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), Aranese (a variety of Gascon) is spoken. It is an official language of [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") together with Catalan and Spanish.
* In [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), Occitan is also spoken in the [Occitan Valleys](/wiki/Occitan_Valleys "Occitan Valleys") ([Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps")) in [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont"). Gardiol also has existed at [Guardia Piemontese](/wiki/Guardia_Piemontese "Guardia Piemontese") ([Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria")) since the 14th century. Italy adopted in 1999 a *Linguistic Minorities Protection Law*, or "Law 482", which includes Occitan; however, [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") is the dominant language. The [Piedmontese language](/wiki/Piedmontese_language "Piedmontese language") is extremely close to Occitan.
* In [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), some Occitan speakers coexist with remaining native speakers of [Monégasque](/wiki/Mon%C3%A9gasque_dialect "Monégasque dialect") ([Ligurian](/wiki/Ligurian_(Romance_language) "Ligurian (Romance language)")). French is the dominant language.
* Scattered Occitan-speaking communities have existed in different countries:
  + There were Occitan-speaking colonies in [Württemberg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg "Württemberg") (Germany) since the 18th century, as a consequence of the [Camisard](/wiki/Camisard "Camisard") war. The last Occitan speakers were heard in the 1930s.
  + In the Spanish [Basque country](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)"), Gascon was spoken in [San Sebastián](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "San Sebastián"), perhaps as late as the early 20th century.[[54]](#cite_note-55)
  + In the Americas, Occitan speakers exist:
    - in the United States, in [Valdese, North Carolina](/wiki/Valdese,_North_Carolina "Valdese, North Carolina")[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57)
    - in Canada, in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") where there are Occitan associations such as *Association Occitane du Québec* and *Association des Occitans*.[[57]](#cite_note-58)
    - [Pigüé, Argentina](/wiki/Pig%C3%BC%C3%A9 "Pigüé") – Community settled by 165 Occitans from the Rodez-Aveyron area of Cantal in the late 19th century.
    - [Guanajuato, Mexico](/wiki/Guanajuato "Guanajuato") – A sparse number of Occitan settlers are known to have settled in that state in the 19th century.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

### Traditionally Occitan-speaking areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Traditionally Occitan-speaking areas")]

* [Aquitaine](/wiki/Aquitaine "Aquitaine") – excluding the Basque-speaking part of the [Pyrénées-Atlantiques](/wiki/Pyr%C3%A9n%C3%A9es-Atlantiques "Pyrénées-Atlantiques") in the western part of the department and a small part of [Gironde](/wiki/Gironde "Gironde") where the *langue d'oïl* [Saintongeais dialect](/wiki/Saintongeais_dialect "Saintongeais dialect") is spoken.
* [Midi-Pyrénées](/wiki/Midi-Pyr%C3%A9n%C3%A9es "Midi-Pyrénées") – including one of France's largest cities, [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse"). There are a few street signs in Toulouse in Occitan, and since late 2009 the [Toulouse Metro](/wiki/Toulouse_Metro "Toulouse Metro") announcements are bilingual French-Occitan,[[59]](#cite_note-60) but otherwise the language is almost never heard spoken on the street.
* [Languedoc-Roussillon](/wiki/Languedoc-Roussillon "Languedoc-Roussillon") (from "Lenga d'òc") – including the areas around the medieval city of [Carcassonne](/wiki/Carcassonne "Carcassonne"), excluding the large part of the [Pyrénées-Orientales](/wiki/Pyr%C3%A9n%C3%A9es-Orientales "Pyrénées-Orientales") where Catalan is spoken ([Fenolheda](/wiki/Fenolheda "Fenolheda") is the only Occitan-speaking area of the Pyrénées-Orientales).
* [Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur](/wiki/Provence-Alpes-C%C3%B4te_d%27Azur "Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur") – except for the Roya and Bévéra valleys, where there is a transitional dialect between Ligurian and Occitan, (*Roiasc*, including the [Brigasc dialect](/wiki/Brigasc_dialect "Brigasc dialect") of Ligurian). In the [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Alpes-Maritimes](/wiki/Alpes-Maritimes "Alpes-Maritimes") there were once isolated towns that spoke [Ligurian](/wiki/Ligurian_(Romance_language) "Ligurian (Romance language)") called Figún,[[60]](#cite_note-61) but those varieties are now extinct. The [Mentonasc dialect](/wiki/Mentonasc_dialect "Mentonasc dialect") of Ligurian, spoken in [Menton](/wiki/Menton "Menton"), is a Ligurian transition dialect with a strong Occitan influence. French is the dominant language of the Alpes-Maritimes, [Dauphiné](/wiki/Dauphin%C3%A9 "Dauphiné") and [French Riviera](/wiki/French_Riviera "French Riviera") areas.
* In [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), Occitan, imported by immigrants coexisted in the 19th and 20th centuries with the [Monégasque dialect](/wiki/Mon%C3%A9gasque_dialect "Monégasque dialect") of Ligurian. [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") is the dominant language.
* [Poitou-Charentes](/wiki/Poitou-Charentes "Poitou-Charentes") – Use of Occitan has declined here in the few parts it used to be spoken, replaced by French. Only Charente Limousine, the eastern part of the region, has resisted. The natural and historical languages of most of the region are the *langues d'oïl* [Poitevin](/wiki/Poitevin_dialect "Poitevin dialect") and Saintongeais.
* [Limousin](/wiki/Limousin "Limousin") – A rural region (about 710,000 inhabitants) where Limousin is still spoken among the oldest residents. French is the dominant language.
* [Auvergne](/wiki/Auvergne_(region) "Auvergne (region)") – The language's use has declined in some urban areas. French is the dominant language. The department of [Allier](/wiki/Allier "Allier") is divided between a southern, Occitan-speaking area and a northern, French-speaking area.
* [Centre-Val de Loire](/wiki/Centre-Val_de_Loire "Centre-Val de Loire") – Some villages in the extreme South speak Occitan.
* [Rhône-Alpes](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne-Alpes "Rhône-Alpes") – While the south of the region is clearly Occitan-speaking, the central and northern [Lyonnais](/wiki/Lyonnais "Lyonnais"), [Forez](/wiki/Forez "Forez") and [Dauphiné](/wiki/Dauphin%C3%A9 "Dauphiné") parts belong to the [Franco-Provençal language](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al_language "Franco-Provençal language") area. French is the dominant language.
* [Occitan Valleys](/wiki/Occitan_Valleys "Occitan Valleys") ([Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont")) – Italian region where Occitan is spoken only in the southern and central Alpine valleys.
* [Val d'Aran](/wiki/Val_d%27Aran "Val d'Aran") – part of Catalonia that speaks a mountain dialect of Gascon.

## Pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Pronunciation")]

The following section describes the pronunciation of the [Languedocian dialect](/wiki/Languedocien_dialect "Languedocien dialect") which is central geographically and the most conservative among Occitan dialects.[[61]](#cite_note-Languedoc-62) For that reason it serves as a basis for standardization of Occitan.[[62]](#cite_note-Standard-63)

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Vowels")]

| Vowel[[63]](#cite_note-Easy-64) | Pronunciation |
| --- | --- |
| a (beginning or in a word); à | [a] |
| a (end of a word); á; ò | [o̞~ɔ, ɛ, e] |
| e, é | [e] |
| è | [ɛ] |
| o, ó | [u~w] |
| i, í | [i] |
| u, ú | [y~ɥ, w] |

### Consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Consonants")]

| Consonant[[63]](#cite_note-Easy-64) | Pronunciation | Consonant | Pronunciation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **b**, **v** ([v] in Northern and Eastern Occitan), **w** | [b~β] | **p** | [p] |
| **c** before a, o, u; **qu** before e and i | [k] | **r**-, **rr** | [r] |
| **c** before e and i; **ç**, **s-**, **-s**, **sc**, **ss, -z** | [s] | **r** inside words; **rn**, **rm** | [ɾ] |
| **cc**; **ts** | [s, ts, ks, kʃ] | -**r** | *silent* |
| **d** | [d~ð] | **t** | [t] |
| **f** | [f] | **x** | [(t)s] |
| **g** before a, o, u; **gu** | [g~ɣ] | **z**; **s** between vowels | [z] |
| **g** before e, i; **j** | [dʒ] | **lh** | [ʎ] |
| -**g** | [k, tʃ] | **nh** | [ɲ] |
| **l**; -**lh** | [l] | **tz** | [ts] |
| **m**; **n** and **m** before p, b and m | [m] | **gn** | [nː] |
| **n**, **nd**, **nt**; -**m** | [n] | **tg**; **tj**; **ch** | [tʃ] |
| **n** before c/qu and g/gu | [ŋ] | **n** and **m** before f | [ɱ] |
| **qu** before a and o | [kw] | **ll**; **tl** | [lː] |

### Stress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Stress")]

Words ending with a vowel or *s*, as well as verb forms ending with *n*, have stress on the penultimate syllable. Words ending with a diphthong or a consonant (except *s* but including *n*) have stress on the last syllable. Exceptions have marked stress.[[63]](#cite_note-Easy-64)

Examples are:

*La mecanica*; destriar; cuélher; cantan; penós; gaton.

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Grammar")]

The following section describes the grammar of the [Languedocian dialect](/wiki/Languedocien_dialect "Languedocien dialect") which is central geographically and most conservative among Occitan dialects.[[61]](#cite_note-Languedoc-62) For that reason it serves as a basis for standardization of Occitan.[[62]](#cite_note-Standard-63)

### Pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Pronouns")]

Personal [pronouns](/wiki/Pronouns "Pronouns") are shown in the following table.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65)

|  | Singular | Plural |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **1st per.** | *Ieu* | *Nosautres / nosautras / nos* |
| **2nd per.** | *Tu* | *Vosautres / vosautras / vos* |
| **3rd per.** | *El (=he)/ela (= she)* | *Eles (masculine), elas (feminine)* |

### Possessives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Possessives")]

[Possessives](/wiki/Possessive "Possessive") are shown in the following table.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65)

|  | Possessed thing is singular... | | Possessed thing is plural... | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Possessor | and masculine | and feminine | and masculine | and feminine |
| I | *Mon* | *Ma* | *Mos* | *Mas* |
| You (sin.) | *Ton* | *Ta* | *Tos* | *Tas* |
| He/ she/ it | *Son* | *Sa* | *Sos* | *Sas* |
| We | *Nòstre* | *Nòstra* | *Nòstres* | *Nòstras* |
| You (pl.) | *Vòstre* | *Vòstra* | *Vòstres* | *Vòstras* |
| They | *Lor* | | *Lors* | |

### Demonstratives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Demonstratives")]

[Demonstratives](/wiki/Demonstratives "Demonstratives") (*this*, *that*, *these*, *those*) are shown in the following table.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65)

|  | Singular | Plural |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Masculine** | *Aiceste/ Aqueste/ Aquel* | *Aicestes/ Aquestes/ Aqueles* |
| **Feminine** | *Aicesta/ Aquesta/ Aquela* | *Aicestas/ Aquestas/ Aquelas* |
| **Neuter** | *Aquò* | *Aquò* |

### Nouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Nouns")]

There are 2 genders: masculine, and feminine. Feminine nouns are usually created by adding termination *-a*.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65) Plural is created by adding *-s* to nouns.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65)

### Articles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Articles")]

Occitan utilizes indefinite articles (compare English "a/an"). There are two indefinite articles in the singular: a masculine form *un* and a feminine form *una, as well as a plural indefinite article*: *de*.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65) The plural form *de* before vowels is shortened to *d'*.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65) Observe the summary in the following table.

| Indefinite articles | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Singular | Plural |
| **Masculine** | *un* | *de*, *d'* |
| **Feminine** | *una* | *de*, *d'* |

Unlike the invariable definite article "the" in English, the definite article of Occitan is inflected for number and gender, as shown in the following table.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65)

| Definite articles | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Singular | Plural |
| **Masculine** | *lo, l'* | *los* |
| **Feminine** | *la, l'* | *las* |

*l'* is used before a vowel in the singular forms.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65)

[Prepositions](/wiki/Preposition "Preposition") *a*, *de*, *per* and *sus* followed by articles *lo* and *los* are merged with them according to the following table.[[64]](#cite_note-Easy:Grammar-65)

|  | lo | los |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **a** | *al* | *als* |
| **de** | *del* | *dels* |
| **per** | *pel* | *pels* |
| **sus** | *sul* | *suls* |

For instance
*a+los = als.*

### Verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Verbs")]

Further information: [Occitan conjugation](/wiki/Occitan_conjugation "Occitan conjugation")

There are 3 [conjugations](/wiki/Grammatical_conjugation "Grammatical conjugation"): *-ar*, *-ir* and -*re*.[[65]](#cite_note-Easy:Verbs-66) Verbs ending with *-ir* have two subconjugations: with and without a suffix.[[65]](#cite_note-Easy:Verbs-66)

### Irregular verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Irregular verbs")]

### Reflexive verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Reflexive verbs")]

Reflexive verbs are verbs which require a [reflexive pronoun](/wiki/Reflexive_pronoun "Reflexive pronoun"). Reflexive pronouns inflect for person and number according to the noun or pronoun to which they refer. An example is *se levar* (= *to get up*). The following table demonstrates the inflection patterns.of the reflexive pronouns.[[65]](#cite_note-Easy:Verbs-66)

|  | Present | Imperative |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Ieu | Me lèvi |  |
| Tu | Te lèvas | lèva-te |
| El/elas | Se lèva |  |
| Nos | Nos levam | levem-nos |
| Vos | Vos levatz | levatz-vos |
| Eles/elas | Se lèvan | tenon |

### Negation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Negation")]

Negation is done by adding *pas* after a verb.[[66]](#cite_note-67) For example:

* *Parli pas* (= *I don't speak*).
* *An pas parlat* (= *They haven't spoken*).
* *Vesi pas res* (= *I don't see anything*).
* *Lo tròbi pas enluòc* (= *I don't find him anywhere*).
* *Sortís pas jamai* (= *He never goes out*).
* *Degun es pas vengut* (= *Nobody came*).

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Dialects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Occitan_dialects_legend-en.svg/500px-Occitan_dialects_legend-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Occitan_dialects_legend-en.svg)

Map of Occitan's six main dialects

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Dialectes_de_l%27occitan_selon_Pierre_Bec.jpg/500px-Dialectes_de_l%27occitan_selon_Pierre_Bec.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dialectes_de_l%27occitan_selon_Pierre_Bec.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Bec_supradialectal.jpg/500px-Bec_supradialectal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bec_supradialectal.jpg)

Supradialectal classification of Occitan according to Bec

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Sumien_supradialectal.jpg/500px-Sumien_supradialectal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sumien_supradialectal.jpg)

Supradialectal classification of Occitan according to Sumien

Occitan is fundamentally defined by its dialects, rather than being a unitary language, as it lacks an official [written standard](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"). Like other languages that fundamentally exist at a spoken, rather than written, level (e.g. the [Rhaeto-Romance languages](/wiki/Rhaeto-Romance_languages "Rhaeto-Romance languages"), [Franco-Provençal](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al_language "Franco-Provençal language"), [Astur-Leonese](/wiki/Astur-Leonese_languages "Astur-Leonese languages"), and [Aragonese](/wiki/Aragonese_language "Aragonese language")), every settlement technically has its own dialect, with the whole of Occitania forming a classic [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") that changes gradually along any path from one side to the other. Nonetheless, specialists commonly divide Occitan into six main dialects:

* [Gascon](/wiki/Gascon_language "Gascon language"): includes the [Béarnese](/wiki/B%C3%A9arnese_dialect "Béarnese dialect") and [Aranese](/wiki/Aranese_dialect "Aranese dialect") (spoken in Spain).
  + [Judeo-Gascon](/wiki/Judeo-Gascon "Judeo-Gascon")
* [Languedocien](/wiki/Languedocien_dialect "Languedocien dialect") (*lengadocian*)
* [Limousin](/wiki/Limousin_dialect "Limousin dialect") (*lemosin*)
* [Auvergnat](/wiki/Auvergnat_dialect "Auvergnat dialect") (*auvernhat*)
* [Provençal](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al_dialect "Provençal dialect") (*provençau* or *prouvençau*), including the [Niçard](/wiki/Ni%C3%A7ois "Niçois") subdialect.
  + [Judeo-Provençal](/wiki/Judeo-Proven%C3%A7al "Judeo-Provençal")
  + [Judeo-Niçard](/w/index.php?title=Judeo-Ni%C3%A7ard&action=edit&redlink=1 "Judeo-Niçard (page does not exist)")
* [Vivaro-Alpine](/wiki/Vivaro-Alpine "Vivaro-Alpine") (*vivaroaupenc*), also known as "Alpine" or "Alpine Provençal", and sometimes considered a subdialect of Provençal

The northern and easternmost dialects have more morphological and phonetic features in common with the [Gallo-Italic](/wiki/Gallo-Italic_languages "Gallo-Italic languages") and [Oïl languages](/wiki/O%C3%AFl_languages "Oïl languages") (e.g. [nasal vowels](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel"); loss of final consonants; initial *cha/ja-* instead of *ca/ga-*; [uvular ⟨r⟩](/wiki/Uvular_R "Uvular R"); the front-rounded sound /ø/ instead of a diphthong, /w/ instead of /l/ before a consonant), whereas the southernmost dialects have more features in common with the [Ibero-Romance languages](/wiki/Ibero-Romance_languages "Ibero-Romance languages") (e.g. [betacism](/wiki/Betacism "Betacism"); voiced fricatives between vowels in place of voiced stops; -*ch*- in place of -*it*-), and Gascon has a number of unusual features not seen in other dialects (e.g. /h/ in place of /f/; loss of /n/ between vowels; intervocalic *-r-* and final *-t/ch* in place of medieval -ll-).

There are also significant lexical differences, where some dialects have words cognate with French, and others have Catalan and Spanish cognates. Nonetheless, there is a significant amount of [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") and some of the words with two cognates can be used in the same dialect as synonymous (*totjorn/sempre* in provençal or *maison/ostau* in gascon for instance).

There is also no particular geographical distribution of the cognates, with some shared by distant dialects and other not shared with bordering foreign languages (for instance *maison* in both Gascon and Niçard, cognate of French but not of Spanish or Italian, although these dialects are geographically closer to these languages).

Occitan words and their French, Catalan and Spanish cognates

| English | Cognate of French | | Cognate of Catalan and Spanish | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Occitan | French | Occitan | Catalan | Spanish |
| house | *maison* | *maison* | *casa* | *casa* | *casa* |
| head | *testa* | *tête* | *cap* | *cap* | *cabeza* |
| to buy | *achaptar* | *acheter* | *crompar* | *comprar* | *comprar* |
| to hear | *entendre* | *entendre* | *ausir* / *audir* | *oir* | *oír* |
| to be quiet | *se taire* | *se taire* | *calar* | *callar* | *callar* |
| to fall | *tombar* | *tomber* | *caire* | *caure* | *caer* |
| more | *pus* | *plus* | *mai* | *més* | *más* |
| always | *totjorn* | *toujours* | *sempre* | *sempre* | *siempre* |
| broom | *balaja* | *balai* | *escoba* | *escombra* | *escoba* |

Gascon is the most divergent, and descriptions of the main features of Occitan often consider Gascon separately. Max Wheeler notes that "probably only its copresence within the French cultural sphere has kept [Gascon] from being regarded as a separate language", and compares it to Franco-Provençal, which is considered a separate language from Occitan but is "probably not more divergent from Occitan overall than Gascon is".[[67]](#cite_note-wheeler-68)

There is no general agreement about larger groupings of these dialects.

Max Wheeler divides the dialects into two groups:[[67]](#cite_note-wheeler-68)

* Southwestern (Gascon and Languedocien), more conservative
* Northeastern (Limousin, Auvergnat, Provençal and Vivaro-Alpine), more innovative

[Pierre Bec](/wiki/P%C3%A8ire_B%C3%A8c "Pèire Bèc") divides the dialects into three groups:[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBec1973-69)

* Gascon, standing alone
* Southern Occitan (Languedocien and Provençal)
* Northern Occitan (Limousin, Auvergnat, Vivaro-Alpine)

In order to overcome the pitfalls of the traditional romanistic view, Bec proposed a "supradialectal" classification that groups Occitan with [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") as a part of a wider Occitano-Romanic group. One such classification posits three groups:[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBec1971472-70)[[70]](#cite_note-MoRS-71)[[71]](#cite_note-OG-72)

* "Arverno-Mediterranean" (*arvèrnomediterranèu*), same as Wheeler's northeastern group, i.e. Limousin, Auvergnat, Provençal and Vivaro-Alpine
* "Central Occitan" (*occitan centrau*), Languedocien, excepting the Southern Languedocien subdialect
* "Aquitano-Pyrenean" (*aquitanopirenenc*), Southern Languedocien, Gascon and Catalan

According to this view, Catalan is an [ausbau language](/wiki/Ausbau_language "Ausbau language") that became independent from Occitan during the 13th century, but originates from the Aquitano-Pyrenean group.

[Domergue Sumien](/wiki/Domergue_Sumien "Domergue Sumien") proposes a slightly different supradialectal grouping.[[72]](#cite_note-sumien2006-73)

* Arverno-Mediterranean (*arvèrnomediterranèu*), same as in Bec and Wheeler, divided further:
  + Niçard-Alpine (*niçardoaupenc*), Vivaro-Alpine along with the Niçard subdialect of Provençal.
  + Trans-Occitan (*transoccitan*), the remainder of Provençal along with Limousin and Auvergnat.
* Pre-Iberian (*preïberic*).
  + Central Occitan (*occitan centrau*), same as in Bec.
  + Aquitano-Pyrenean (*aquitanopirenenc*), same as in Bec.

### Jewish dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Jewish dialects")]

Occitan has 3 dialects spoken by Jewish communities that are all now extinct.

#### Judeo-Gascon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Judeo-Gascon")]

A [sociolect](/wiki/Sociolect "Sociolect") of the Gascon dialect spoken by [Spanish and Portuguese Jews](/wiki/Spanish_and_Portuguese_Jews "Spanish and Portuguese Jews") in [Gascony](/wiki/Gascony "Gascony").[[73]](#cite_note-74) It, like many other Jewish dialects and languages, contained large amounts of [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") loanwords.[[74]](#cite_note-75) It went extinct after [World War 2](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") with the last speakers being elderly Jews in [Bayonne](/wiki/Bayonne "Bayonne"). About 850 unique words and a few morphological and grammatical aspects of the dialect were transmitted to Southern Jewish French.[[75]](#cite_note-:0-76)

#### Judeo-Provençal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Judeo-Provençal")]

[Judeo-Provençal](/wiki/Judeo-Proven%C3%A7al "Judeo-Provençal") was a dialect of Occitan spoken by Jews in [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence"). The dialect declined in usage after Jews were expelled from the area in 1498, and was probably extinct by the 20th century.

#### Judeo-Niçard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Judeo-Niçard")]

The least attested of the Judeo-Occitan dialects, Judeo-Niçard was spoken by the community of Jews living in [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice"), who were descendants of Jewish immigrants from Provence, Piedmont, and other Mediterranean communities. Its existence is attested from a few documents from the 19th century. It contained significant influence in both vocabulary and grammar from Hebrew.[[75]](#cite_note-:0-76)

#### Southern Jewish French

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Southern Jewish French")]

All three of these dialects have some influence in Southern Jewish French, a dialect of French spoken by Jews in southern France. Southern Jewish French is now estimated to only be spoken by about 50–100 people.[[75]](#cite_note-:0-76)

## Codification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Codification")]

### Standardization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Standardization")]

All regional varieties of the Occitan language have a written form; thus, Occitan can be considered as a [pluricentric language](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language"). Standard Occitan, also called *occitan larg* (i.e., 'wide Occitan') is a synthesis that respects and admits soft regional adaptations (which are based on the convergence of previous regional [koinés](/wiki/Koin%C3%A9_language "Koiné language")).[[72]](#cite_note-sumien2006-73) The standardization process began with the publication of *[Gramatica occitana segon los parlars lengadocians](/w/index.php?title=Gramatica_occitana_segon_los_parlars_lengadocians&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gramatica occitana segon los parlars lengadocians (page does not exist)")* ("Grammar of the Languedocien Dialect") by [Louis Alibert](/wiki/Louis_Alibert "Louis Alibert") (1935), followed by the *[Dictionnaire occitan-français selon les parlers languedociens](/w/index.php?title=Dictionnaire_occitan-fran%C3%A7ais_selon_les_parlers_languedociens&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dictionnaire occitan-français selon les parlers languedociens (page does not exist)")* ("French-Occitan dictionary according to Languedocien") by the same author (1966), completed during the 1970s with the works of [Pierre Bec](/wiki/Pierre_Bec "Pierre Bec") (Gascon), [Robèrt Lafont](/wiki/Rob%C3%A8rt_Lafont "Robèrt Lafont") (Provençal), and others. However, the process has not yet been completed as of the present.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Standardization is mostly supported by users of the [classical norm](#Writing_system). Due to the strong situation of [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia "Diglossia"), some users[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] thusly reject the standardization process, and do not conceive Occitan as a language that can be standardized as per other standardized languages.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Writing system")]

Further information: [Occitan alphabet](/wiki/Occitan_alphabet "Occitan alphabet")

There are two main linguistic norms currently used for Occitan, one (known as "classical") based on that of Medieval Occitan, and one (sometimes known as "Mistralian", due to its use by [Frédéric Mistral](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Mistral "Frédéric Mistral")) based on modern [French orthography](/wiki/French_orthography "French orthography"). Sometimes, there is conflict between users of each system.

* The *classical norm* (or less exactly *classical orthography*) has the advantage of maintaining a link with earlier stages of the language, and reflects the fact that Occitan is not a variety of French. It is used in all Occitan dialects. It also allows speakers of one dialect of Occitan to write intelligibly for speakers of other dialects (e.g. the Occitan for *day* is written *jorn* in the classical norm, but could be *jour*, *joun*, *journ*, or even *yourn*, depending on the writer's origin, in Mistralian orthography). The Occitan classical orthography and the [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") orthography are quite similar: they show the very close ties of both languages. The digraphs *lh* and *nh*, used in the classical orthography, were adopted by the [orthography of Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_alphabet#Basic_digraphs "Portuguese alphabet"), presumably by [Gerald of Braga](/wiki/Gerald_of_Braga "Gerald of Braga"), a monk from [Moissac](/wiki/Moissac "Moissac"), who became bishop of [Braga](/wiki/Braga "Braga") in Portugal in 1047, playing a major role in modernizing written [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") using classical Occitan norms.[[77]](#cite_note-78)
* The *[Mistralian norm](/wiki/Mistralian_norm "Mistralian norm")* (or less exactly *Mistralian orthography*) has the advantage of being similar to that of French, in which most Occitan speakers are literate. Now, it is used mostly in the [Provençal](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al_(dialect) "Provençal (dialect)")/[Niçard](/wiki/Ni%C3%A7ard "Niçard") dialect, besides the classical norm. It has also been used by a number of eminent writers, particularly in Provençal. However, it is somewhat impractical, because it is based mainly on the Provençal dialect and also uses many digraphs for simple sounds, the most notable one being *ou* for the [u] sound, as it is in French, written as *o* under the classical orthography.

There are also two other norms but they have a lesser audience. The *Escòla dau Pò norm* (or *Escolo dóu Po norm*) is a simplified version of the Mistralian norm and is used only in the Occitan Valleys (Italy), besides the classical norm. The *Bonnaudian norm* (or *écriture auvergnate unifiée, EAU*) was created by Pierre Bonnaud and is used only in the [Auvergnat](/wiki/Auvergnat "Auvergnat") dialect, besides the classical norm.

### Orthography IETF subtags

Several [IETF language subtags](/wiki/IETF_language_tag "IETF language tag") have been registered for the different orthographies:[[76]](#cite_note-IETF-77)

* `oc-grital`: Italian-inspired Occitan orthography.
* `oc-grmistr`: Mistralian-inspired Occitan orthography.

### Debates concerning linguistic classification and orthography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Debates concerning linguistic classification and orthography")]

The majority of scholars think that Occitan constitutes a single language.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKremnitz2002109–111-81) Some authors,[[81]](#cite_note-82) constituting a minority,[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKremnitz2002109–111-81) reject this opinion and even the name *Occitan*, thinking that there is a family of distinct *lengas d'òc* rather than dialects of a single language.

Many Occitan linguists and writers,[[82]](#cite_note-83) particularly those involved with the pan-Occitan movement centered on the [Institut d'Estudis Occitans](/wiki/Institut_d%27Estudis_Occitans "Institut d'Estudis Occitans"), disagree with the view that Occitan is a family of languages; instead they believe Limousin, Auvergnat, Languedocien, Gascon, Provençal and Vivaro-Alpine are dialects of a single language. Although there are indeed noticeable differences between these varieties, there is a very high degree of [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") between them[[83]](#cite_note-84) partly because they share a common literary history; furthermore, academic and literary circles have identified them as a collective linguistic entity—the *lenga d'òc*—for centuries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some Provençal authors continue to support the view that Provençal is a separate language.[[84]](#cite_note-85) Nevertheless, the vast majority of Provençal authors and associations think that Provençal is a part of Occitan.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

This debate about the status of Provençal should not be confused with the debate concerning the spelling of Provençal.

* The **classical orthography** is [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic_orthography "Phonemic orthography") and [diasystemic](/wiki/Diasystem "Diasystem"), and thus more pan-Occitan. It can be used for (and adapted to) all Occitan dialects and regions, including Provençal. Its supporters think that Provençal is a part of Occitan.
* The **Mistralian orthography** of Provençal is more or less phonemic but not diasystemic and is closer to the [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") spelling and therefore more specific to Provençal; its users are divided between the ones who think that Provençal is a part of Occitan and the ones who think that Provençal is a separate language.

For example, the classical system writes *Polonha*, whereas the Mistralian spelling system has *Poulougno*, for [puˈluɲo], 'Poland'.

The question of [Gascon](/wiki/Gascon_language "Gascon language") is similar. Gascon presents a number of significant differences from the rest of the language; but, despite these differences, Gascon and other Occitan dialects have very important common lexical and grammatical features, so authors such as Pierre Bec argue that they could never be considered as different as, for example, Spanish and Italian.[[86]](#cite_note-87) In addition, Gascon's being included in Occitan despite its particular differences can be justified because there is a common elaboration (*[Ausbau](/wiki/Ausbau_language "Ausbau language")*) process between Gascon and the rest of Occitan.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKremnitz2002109–111-81) The vast majority of the Gascon cultural movement considers itself as a part of the Occitan cultural movement.[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89) And the official status of [Val d'Aran](/wiki/Val_d%27Aran "Val d'Aran") ([Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), Spain), adopted in 1990, says that [Aranese](/wiki/Aranese "Aranese") is a part of Gascon and Occitan. A grammar of Aranese by Aitor Carrera, published in 2007 in [Lleida](/wiki/Lleida "Lleida"), presents the same view.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarrera2007-90)

The exclusion of [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") from the Occitan sphere, even though Catalan is closely related, is justified because there has been a consciousness of its being different from Occitan since the later Middle Ages and because the elaboration (*Ausbau*) processes of Catalan and Occitan (including Gascon) have been quite distinct since the 20th century. Nevertheless, other scholars point out that the process that led to the affirmation of Catalan as a distinct language from Occitan started during the period when the pressure to include Catalan-speaking areas in a mainstream Spanish culture was at its greatest.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

The answer to the question of whether Gascon or [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") should be considered dialects of Occitan or separate languages has long been a matter of opinion or convention, rather than based on scientific ground. However, two recent studies support Gascon's being considered a distinct language. For the first time, a quantifiable, statistics-based approach was applied by Stephan Koppelberg in attempt to solve this issue.[[91]](#cite_note-92) Based on the results he obtained, he concludes that Catalan, Occitan, and Gascon should all be considered three distinct languages. More recently, Y. Greub and J.P. Chambon (Sorbonne University, Paris) demonstrated that the formation of Proto-Gascon was already complete at the eve of the 7th century, whereas Proto-Occitan was not yet formed at that time.[[92]](#cite_note-93) These results induced linguists to do away with the conventional classification of Gascon, favoring the "distinct language" alternative.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Both studies supported the early intuition of the late [Kurt Baldinger](/wiki/Kurt_Baldinger "Kurt Baldinger"), a specialist of both medieval Occitan and medieval Gascon, who recommended that Occitan and Gascon be classified as separate languages.[[93]](#cite_note-94)[[94]](#cite_note-95)

## Linguistic characterization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Linguistic characterization")]

Further information: [Occitan phonology](/wiki/Occitan_phonology "Occitan phonology")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section includes a list of [general references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#General_references "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), but **it lacks sufficient corresponding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help to [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Reliability "Wikipedia:WikiProject Reliability") this section by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(July 2011)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Jules Ronjat has sought to characterize Occitan with 19 principal, generalizable criteria. Of those, 11 are phonetic, five morphologic, one syntactic, and two lexical. For example, close rounded vowels are rare or absent in Occitan. This characteristic often carries through to an Occitan speaker's French, leading to a distinctive *[méridional](/wiki/Meridional_French "Meridional French")* accent. Unlike French, it is a [pro-drop language](/wiki/Pro-drop_language "Pro-drop language"), allowing the omission of the subject (*canti*: I sing; *cantas* you sing)—though, at least in Gascon, the verb must be preceded by an "enunciative" in place of the pronoun, *e* for questions, *be* for observations, *que* for other occasions: e.g., *que soi* (I am), *E qu'ei?* (He/she is?), *Be qu'èm.* (We are.).[[95]](#cite_note-96) Among these 19 discriminating criteria, 7 are different from Spanish, 8 from Italian, 12 from Franco-Provençal, and 16 from French.

### Features of Occitan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Features of Occitan")]

Most features of Occitan are shared with either [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") or [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), or both.

#### Features of Occitan as a whole

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Features of Occitan as a whole")]

Examples of pan-Occitan features shared with French, but not Catalan:

* Latin ū [uː] (Vulgar Latin /u/) changed to /y/, as in French (Lat. dv̄rvm > Oc. *dur*).
* Vulgar Latin /o/ changed to /u/, first in unstressed syllables, as in Eastern Catalan (Lat. romānvs > Oc. *roman* [ruˈma]), then in stressed syllables (Lat. flōrem > Oc. *flor* [fluɾ]).

Examples of pan-Occitan features shared with Catalan, but not French:

* Stressed Latin a was preserved (Lat. mare > Oc. *mar*, Fr. *mer*).
* Intervocalic -t- was lenited to /d/ rather than lost (Lat. vitam > Oc. *vida*, Fr. *vie*).

Examples of pan-Occitan features not shared with Catalan or French:

* Original /aw/ preserved.
* Final /a/ becomes /ɔ/ (note in [Valencian](/wiki/Valencian_language "Valencian language") (Catalan), /ɔ/ may appear in word-final unstressed position, in a process of [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony")).
* Low-mid /ɛ/ and /ɔ/ diphthongized before velars. /ɛ/ generally becomes /jɛ/; /ɔ/ originally became /wɔ/ or /wɛ/, but has since usually undergone further fronting (e.g. to [ɥɛ], [ɥɔ], [jɔ], [œ], [ɛ], [ɥe], [we], etc.). Diphthongization also occurred before palatals, as in French and Catalan.
* Various assimilations in consonant clusters (e.g. ⟨cc⟩ in *Occitan*, pronounced /utsiˈta/ in conservative Languedocien).

#### Features of some Occitan dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Occitan_language&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Features of some Occitan dialects")]

Examples of dialect-specific features of the northerly dialects shared with French, but not Catalan:

* Palatalization of ca-, ga- to /tʃa, dʒa/.
* Vocalization of syllable-final /l/ to /w/.
* Loss of final consonants.
* Vocalization of syllable-final nasals to [nasal vowels](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel").
* [Uvularization](/wiki/Uvular_R "Uvular R") of some or all ⟨r⟩ sounds.

Examples of dialect-specific features of the southerly dialects (or some of them) shared with Catalan, but not French:

* Latin -mb-,-nd- become /m, n/.
* [Betacism](/wiki/Betacism "Betacism"): /b/ and /v/ merge (feature shared with Spanish and some Catalan dialects; except for Balearic, Valencian and Algherese Catalan, where /v/ is preserved).
* Intervocalic voiced stops /b d ɡ/ (from Latin -p-, -t, -c-) become voiced fricatives [β ð ɣ].
* Loss of word-final single /n/ (but not /nn/, e.g. *an* "year" < ānnvm).

Examples of [Gascon](/wiki/Gascon_language "Gascon language")-specific features not shared with French or Catalan:

* Latin initial /f/ changed into /h/ (Lat. filivm > Gasc. *hilh*). This also happened in [medieval Spanish](/wiki/History_of_Spanish "History of Spanish"), although the /h/ was eventually lost, or reverted to /f/ (before a consonant). The Gascon ⟨h⟩ has retained its [aspiration](/wiki/Aspiration_(phonetics) "Aspiration (phonetics)").
* Loss of /n/ between vowels. This also happened in [Portuguese](/wiki/History_of_Portuguese "History of Portuguese") and [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") (and moreover also in [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language")).
* Change of -ll- to ⟨r⟩ /ɾ/, or ⟨th⟩ word-finally (originally the [voiceless palatal stop](/wiki/Voiceless_palatal_stop "Voiceless palatal stop") /c/, but now generally either /t/ or /tʃ/, depending on the word). This is a unique characteristic of Gascon and of certain [Aragonese](/wiki/Aragonese_language "Aragonese language") dialects.

Examples of other dialect-specific features not shared with French or Catalan:

* Merging of syllable-final nasals to /ŋ/. This appears to represent a transitional stage before nasalization, and occurs especially in the southerly dialects other than Gascon (which still maintains different final nasals, as in Catalan).
* Former intervocalic /ð/ (from Latin -d-) becomes /z/ (most dialects, but not Gascon). This appears to have happened in primitive Catalan as well, but Catalan later deleted this sound or converted it to /w/.
* Palatalization of /jt/ (from Latin ct) to /tʃ/ in most dialects or /(j)t/: *lach* vs *lait* (Gascon *lèit*) 'milk', *lucha* vs *luta* (Gascon *luta*) 'fight'.
* Weakening of /l/ to /r/ in the Vivaro-Alpine dialect.
